title: refactor(gen-audio): remove dead speed param, stale comment, and dedupe transcription response builder
author: voodoohop
pr_body_excerpt:
- Drop unimplemented `speed` field from CreateSpeechRequestSchema (POST /speech); no handler read it and it was never forwarded to any provider.
- Rewrite dangling comment that named the deleted `parseMp4Duration` function; duration is estimated from MP3 byte size.
- Extract shared `buildTranscriptionResponse` (transcription-response.ts) emitting the text/verbose_json/diarized_json/json branches; ElevenLabs and AssemblyAI keep their own normalization (groupScribeUtterances; ms->s + field fallbacks) and map into a seconds-based intermediate.

Risks:
- Out-of-range `speed` is now silently stripped instead of returning 400; never affected generated audio (the param was unused).
- `speed` leaves the inferred CreateSpeechRequest type and the POST /speech OpenAPI docs (intended); polli-cli `--speed` still sends an ignored query param (follow-up, out of scope).

_~138 net lines removed · biome ✓ · cd gen.pollinations.ai && npx vitest run test/scribe-transcription.test.ts test/assemblyai ✓_

gist.headline: Trimming the audio weeds
gist.blurb: We did some spring cleaning in the audio generation code, removing unused parameters and streamlining how transcriptions are built. A cleaner codebase means faster updates in the future!
gist.summary: Removed the unimplemented `speed` parameter from the POST /speech endpoint and extracted a shared `buildTranscriptionResponse` utility for ElevenLabs and AssemblyAI.
gist.impact: Developers will see a cleaner OpenAPI schema without the dead `speed` field, while internal transcription handling is now more maintainable.
gist.visual_concept: Polly the bee and the Nomnom creature doing some spring cleaning. Nomnom eating dead code (the unused speed parameter) while Polly organizes audio files.
gist.image_prompt: Cozy pixel art scene of the cute bee mascot wearing a tiny bandana, organizing retro audio cassettes on a wooden shelf. Nearby, the round tan Nomnom creature happily eats a dusty piece of paper labeled 'SPEED'. Soft lime green (#ecf874) and lavender gradient background. Chunky pixels, warm lighting, lo-fi aesthetic like Stardew Valley. Emotionally warm, nostalgic but beautiful.
